title: Logistics Management Platform
subtitle: Production B2B/B2C courier logistics platform — three role-based interfaces, real-time SSE notifications, automated PDF waybills, and dynamic pricing engine
problem: The client was operating a courier business across multiple cities with no unified system — orders were tracked via Telegram, couriers reported status by phone, managers had no real-time visibility into delivery progress, and financial reports were assembled manually in Excel. There was no way to assign orders dynamically, monitor delays, or generate waybill documents without significant manual work. The absence of a structured platform was directly costing the business in missed deliveries and untracked revenue.
solution: Designed and built a full-stack logistics platform as a monorepo serving three distinct user roles from a single backend. Clients submit delivery requests with Google Maps address autocomplete and track their orders live. Couriers work from a PWA-optimized mobile interface — picking up orders, updating statuses, viewing route maps, and tracking earnings in real time. Managers get a CRM dashboard with order monitoring, courier performance analytics, configurable price matrix, delay alerts, and one-click PDF waybill generation. All role interfaces share a single Node.js/Express backend with JWT role-based middleware protecting every route.
architecture: Monorepo with three layers: a single Next.js frontend with role-based routing groups (client), (courier), (manager), a Node.js/Express backend following a layered Routes → Controllers → Services pattern, and a Supabase PostgreSQL database with custom triggers and Row-Level Security. The backend exposes a unified API Gateway with six controller domains: Auth, Order, Courier, Notification, Document, and City/Pricing. Real-time updates are delivered via Server-Sent Events (SSE) — a lightweight unidirectional stream from server to client used for order status changes and courier assignment notifications, with no WebSocket infrastructure overhead. Redis caches heavy courier stat aggregations (earnings, delivery history, monthly rankings) to avoid repeated costly DB aggregation queries. PDF waybill generation runs entirely server-side with embedded NotoSans Cyrillic fonts — no third-party document service. Dynamic pricing is calculated via a configurable price matrix (zone × weight × distance) with Google Maps Autocomplete for address validation and geocoding. The backend is containerized via Docker and deployed to Fly.io; the frontend runs on Vercel. Full interactive API documentation is auto-generated via Swagger / OpenAPI 3.0 with JWT auth support at /api-docs.
result: Full courier operations lifecycle covered end-to-end — from client order submission to delivery confirmation and financial reporting. Three completely independent role interfaces served from a single codebase with zero role data leakage via JWT middleware. SSE notifications deliver order status updates with under 1s latency without WebSocket infrastructure. Redis caching reduces database load on courier stat endpoints by eliminating repeated aggregation queries. PDF waybills with Cyrillic support generated in under 500ms. Google Maps integration provides address autocomplete and geocoding across all cities. The platform is currently in active development for production launch — video demo available upon request.
ndaNote: Client project under NDA. Source code is private. Architecture diagrams and video demo available upon request.

title: Headless E-Commerce Platform
subtitle: Next.js storefront powered by WordPress + WooCommerce as a headless backend — full checkout flow, JWT auth, product reviews, SEO optimization, and Cloudinary image delivery
problem: Traditional WooCommerce themes are slow, hard to customize, and score poorly on Core Web Vitals. The WordPress rendering pipeline limits frontend flexibility, making it difficult to build modern, fast storefronts without fighting the theme system. Product pages regularly scored under 50 on Lighthouse, and every UI change required PHP knowledge.
solution: Decoupled the frontend entirely — Next.js handles all rendering while WooCommerce runs purely as a headless backend via REST API. A BFF layer via Next.js API Routes handles all transactional flows server-side: JWT login, registration, forgot password, order creation, order history and review submission — keeping credentials out of the browser. JWT tokens are verified server-side via jsonwebtoken. Products and blog posts are fetched with smart SSG/ISR/SSR per page type. Route Groups organise the app into clean layout boundaries — (auth), (shop), (pages), (landing) — without affecting URLs. Cloudinary delivers optimized product images via next/image.
architecture: Three-layer architecture: Next.js App Router as the storefront, Next.js API Routes as a BFF layer, and WordPress + WooCommerce as a fully headless backend. Server Components call WooCommerce REST API (/wc/v3/*) and WordPress REST API (/wp/v2/*) directly with Next.js fetch caching — product pages use ISR (revalidate: 3600s), category and search pages use SSR, blog is fully static. BFF API routes: auth (login, register, forgot-password), order creation with optional customer linking, authenticated order history, and product reviews — all with server-side JWT verification via jsonwebtoken. Client state is handled by three React Contexts — cart in sessionStorage, favourites and auth token in localStorage. SEO is centralised in lib/seo.ts — site-wide metadata template, per-page static metadata, and dynamic product metadata generation.
result: Lighthouse Performance 94 / SEO 100 on static product pages. LCP under 2.5s. Complete e-commerce lifecycle — catalog, filtering, product detail, reviews, checkout, order confirmation, order history, blog, discounts with coupons, wishlist, forgot password. Zero PHP knowledge required to customise the storefront — pure TypeScript/React. WooCommerce admin remains fully functional for content editors. Guest and authenticated checkout both supported — orders linked to customer account when JWT is present.

title: Zere AI
subtitle: Commercial SaaS — AI-powered university assistant with multilingual semantic search and real-time streaming chat
problem: University support staff were spending the majority of their day answering the same recurring questions — exam retake procedures, tuition deadlines, academic schedules, department contacts. The knowledge existed across hundreds of PDFs and internal documents, but there was no fast way to surface it. Students waited hours for answers that were already written down somewhere.
solution: Built a white-label SaaS platform that ingests a university's document base, indexes it via multilingual vector embeddings, and delivers answers in real-time through a streaming WebSocket chat. The system handles Kazakh, Russian, and English in the same knowledge base without configuration. Administrators get a CRM panel to manage student groups and bulk-import rosters from Excel. The entire stack runs in Docker Compose and can be deployed to any university with a single environment file.
architecture: Three-service monorepo: Next.js frontend, Express.js backend, and a FastAPI microservice for semantic search — all coordinated via Docker Compose on a single port. The backend exposes one WebSocket endpoint at /ws. When a question arrives, the server emits a typed event stream: searching → search_done → generating → token (×N) → done. FastAPI retrieves the top-3 most relevant document chunks using cosine similarity on multilingual sentence embeddings, with its own Redis cache for search results (TTL 5m). The answer is generated by Groq API with stream: true — each SSE chunk is forwarded to the WebSocket client immediately, so the first token appears in under 200ms. Completed LLM answers are cached in Redis by a SHA-256 hash of the question and search context (TTL 1h), and replayed token-by-token on cache hit to preserve the streaming UX. Server-side sessions are stored in-memory (TTL 2h) and pass the last 10 messages as conversation context to the LLM on each turn.
result: First token in under 200ms on a live Groq call; under 500ms on Redis cache hit. Redis caching cuts estimated LLM API cost by 40–60% on repeated questions. The multilingual model resolves queries in Kazakh, Russian, and English without any language-routing logic. The CRM panel supports bulk Excel imports with automatic IIN column detection and filtering, preventing accidental exposure of national ID numbers. Containerised and documented for handoff to university IT departments with a one-command Docker Compose setup.

title: Satpayev Science TZ
subtitle: Hackathon project — AI platform for analyzing and improving scientific technical specifications at Satbayev University
problem: Research teams at Kazakh universities submit scientific technical specifications (TZ) that are evaluated manually by expert committees. The process is slow, subjective, and inconsistent — reviewers apply the official scoring rubric differently, feedback arrives weeks after submission, and researchers have no way to self-assess quality before sending. Low-quality submissions waste committee time and delay funding decisions.
solution: Built an end-to-end AI platform that accepts a researcher's TZ document, runs multi-dimensional NLP analysis, drafts a scored evaluation using the official 100-point rubric, and provides an AI chat assistant grounded in the specific document and analysis results. Admins get a review panel to edit AI-drafted scorecards, write expert feedback, and export approved evaluations to the official XLSX format. The platform was designed and shipped within a single hackathon at Satbayev University, Almaty.
architecture: Four-service monorepo: Next.js frontend, Go/Fiber core backend, Python/FastAPI AI service, and a Terraform-managed AWS infrastructure module. The architecture enforces a strict contract: Core Backend is the only system of record — it owns auth, projects, document versions, analysis runs, scorecards, chat, and reports. The AI Service is a stateless intelligence engine with no direct database access; it receives structured context from the backend and returns analysis results over HTTP. The frontend never calls the AI Service directly. When a researcher uploads a TZ document, the backend extracts text, persists a versioned document record, and triggers an analysis run. The AI Service performs multi-dimensional analysis across five diagnostic dimensions (structure, completeness, clarity, KPI results, consistency) and drafts a preliminary evaluation scorecard using the official academic rubric across seven categories (strategic relevance, scientific novelty, practical applicability, and four more — total 100 points). Findings, recommendations, and scorecard items are all persisted in PostgreSQL so the chat assistant can reference them without re-running inference. The chat system loads project metadata, extracted text, latest analysis, both scorecards, and prior messages as context before each AI call. Cloud deployment uses Terraform + EC2 + Docker Compose with runtime secrets injected from AWS Secrets Manager; the frontend is served through CloudFront, and S3 handles uploaded documents and generated report artifacts.
result: Delivered a working full-stack platform in one hackathon sprint. The system covers the complete researcher workflow: upload → AI analysis → scorecard preview → AI chat → submission → admin review → XLSX export. The scoring model mirrors the official committee rubric exactly, making AI-drafted scores directly comparable to human expert scores. The infrastructure is fully reproducible: one Terraform apply and one deploy script brings up the entire AWS stack from scratch with secrets managed outside the repository.
